problem_summary: 2016 MCM Problem B (Discrete): Find an economically attractive commercial opportunity that a private firm could adopt to address the growing space-debris problem, and build a model to determine the best alternative (or combination) for doing so. This team's central move is to reframe the open-ended "commercial opportunity" prompt as a concrete business: a space-debris collision INSURANCE service whose claims risk is actively lowered by renting existing Active Debris Removal (ADR) technology. They then build a quantitative pipeline that prices premiums from physics-based collision risk and chooses ADR methods to maximize expected profit E = premium*(1-risk) - claims*risk - ADR_cost.
models_used: Debris-flux / collision-risk model built on NASA ORDEM 2000 (Orbital Debris Engineering Model) via the DAS 2.0 software, restricted to LEO; flux F = ∂²q/(∂t∂S), orbit-average flux F̄ = N/T
Annual collision probability via the ESA Space Debris Mitigation cross-sectional-sweep formula: P_c,yr = Σ_j F̄_j · (π/4)(D_t + D_p,j)², summed over discrete debris diameter bins

Effective (catastrophic) collision probability P_e = P_c,yr − P_in, using an energy-to-mass-ratio (EMR) catastrophic threshold of 40 J/g to derive a critical debris diameter D_p,c = sqrt[(8/π)(A_p/M_p)_std (EMR)_cc M_t / V_imp²]; only debris above D_p,c counts as effective

Insurance pricing model: traditional actuarial form α = C_p/C_z, α ≈ (1.2~1.5)β; then a custom 'improved' premium-rate law α = (8 + log₁₀ β̂)/100 with β̂ = P_e, mapping collision probability (10⁻⁵–10⁻³) onto a ~3–5% premium rate
Expected-profit optimization with a critical-failure-probability threshold γ: E = C_z(1−γ) − C_p·γ − cost(β̂, γ, t), where ADR is triggered only when β̂ > γ

Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation (FCE) for ADR method selection: weight vector A = [0.15, 0.35, 0.2, 0.3] over factors {manufacture cost, cost of using, fitness, time}, judgment matrix R, score B = A·R; evaluated separately per debris size class (small/medium/large) and per region (normal vs. hardest-hit altitude bands)

Greedy term-dropping heuristic on the discrete P_c,yr sum: repeatedly remove the diameter bin with the largest F̄_j·(π/4)(D_t+D_p,j)² contribution until P_e falls below threshold (defines which debris sizes to clean)
Monte-Carlo-style end-to-end simulation in MATLAB + DAS 2.0 across three representative scenarios (hardest-hit satellite, ISS-class station above 400 km, satellite outside the hardest-hit band)
key_techniques: Exploiting the DISCRETE additive structure of the risk sum: because P_c,yr is a sum over debris-diameter bins, each term is an independently attributable, removable risk contribution — this single observation links the physics model directly to the ADR decision (remove the highest-contribution bins first)
Using a published engineering model (ORDEM 2000 / DAS) as a real data source instead of inventing debris numbers — grounds the whole pipeline in NASA observational data

Two-stage decomposition of the ADR problem: Step 1 = WHICH sizes to remove (greedy on risk contribution), Step 2 = WHICH technology to use (FCE) — cleanly separates a continuous-threshold question from a discrete-choice question

Borrowing the catastrophic-collision EMR=40 J/g criterion from ESA guidelines to define 'effective' vs 'ineffective' collisions, so the model prices only damage-causing impacts rather than all impacts
Designing a bespoke premium-rate curve (log-linear in collision probability, anchored to the real-world ~8% industry rate) so that pricing is monotone, bounded, and physically interpretable
Region- and size-stratified FCE matrices (normal vs hardest-hit 700–1000 km and 1400–1600 km bands) so the method recommendation adapts to local debris density
Closing the loop with a profit equation that nets premium income against expected claims AND ADR spend, making 'is this a good business?' an explicit optimization rather than a narrative claim
why_it_won: "From a judge's perspective, the winning quality is a fully closed, physics-to-dollars pipeline that answers the actual prompt. The prompt asks for a commercial opportunity and a model to pick the best alternative; this team delivers a profitable BUSINESS (insurance + rented ADR), not just a debris-cleanup scheme, and proves profitability with concrete dollar figures (e.g., +3.04M, +780M, +3.66M across three scenarios). Crucially they ground risk in a real, citable NASA model (ORDEM 2000/DAS) and a real ESA collision-risk formula, so the numbers feel credible rather than fabricated. The discrete additive risk sum is leveraged elegantly: the same equation that prices the premium also tells you which debris to remove, giving the paper a tight internal logic. The two-step ADR design (size threshold via greedy term-dropping, then technology choice via FCE) maps the messy 'best alternative or combination' question onto two tractable sub-models. Sensitivity analysis over premium rate, insurance duration, and FCE weights demonstrates robustness and directly addresses the 'What if?' requirement. Finally, the executive summary and policy/action recommendations show the team answering the real-world decision-maker, which ICM judges reward."
section_highlights: "Structure follows the canonical winning template and reinforces the central narrative at every level. Strong elements: (1) A summary sheet that states the business idea, the risk model, the ADR strategy, and the profitability conclusion in one page. (2) A repeated model-schematic flowchart (Fig 3/7 and the exec-summary version) that visually closes the loop premium↔claims↔risk↔ADR cost, so the reader always sees how a section fits the whole. (3) A clean notation table up front. (4) Explicit, listed General Assumptions (inclination ignored, altitude fixed, only debris causes damage, large-numbers law holds) that bound the model honestly. (5) Worked numerical scenarios in Section 8 with specific satellite mass/size/cost inputs and resulting D_p,c, P_e, premium rate, post-ADR probability, and profit — concrete enough to audit. (6) A dedicated Strengths/Weaknesses section that self-critiques (data gaps, ignored inclination, no tech-progress modeling). (7) A separate Executive Summary with a Problem Background, Business Model, ADR recommendation, and Action Recommendation aimed at policymakers — exactly the ICM deliverable style. The writing keeps a single throughline ('does this make money, and how do we pick ADR?') from summary to conclusion."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Cost side is essentially un-sourced: ADR manufacture/launch costs and the 'value(strategy)' and 'fee(strategy)' terms are estimated, so the headline profit figures (3.04M, 780M, 3.66M) rest on guessed cost inputs — the team admits this under Weaknesses
The improved premium-rate law α = (8 + log₁₀ β̂)/100 is essentially fitted by hand to hit ~3–5% and the industry 8% anchor; it has no actuarial derivation and its constants are unjustified

FCE judgment matrices R are populated with subjective 0–1 scores the team assigned themselves; the recommendations (laser for small, large satellite for large) are only as good as those hand-picked entries, and no source backs them
Key physics simplifications: orbit inclination ignored, altitude held constant, HEO neglected, all collisions assumed independent — each can materially change real flux and risk
The greedy term-dropping heuristic for choosing which size bins to remove is not proven optimal; it is a local rule, not a true optimization of cost vs risk reduction

Premium magnitudes in Section 8 look internally inconsistent / implausible (e.g., a 981M premium on a 100B station vs 7.26M on a 0.5B satellite), suggesting unit or scaling looseness in the worked numbers
Sensitivity analysis on the FCE factor is essentially waved off ('this page will not focus on this') rather than quantified, weakening the robustness claim for the most subjective part of the model
OCR/transcription artifacts aside, the catastrophic-collision and effective-probability derivation is presented quickly and would benefit from a sanity-check against known on-orbit collision rates
reusable_patterns: Reframe an open 'find a commercial opportunity' prompt into a concrete revenue-vs-cost business object with an explicit profit equation E = income − expected_loss − mitigation_cost; optimize that, don't just narrate feasibility

Anchor risk in a real, citable engineering model + its software (here ORDEM 2000 / DAS) so quantities come from data, not invention — search for the field's standard model before building your own from scratch
When a risk/quantity is a SUM over discrete bins, exploit per-term attribution: the same sum can price risk AND drive a greedy 'remove the biggest contributors first' decision rule

Split a 'choose the best alternative or combination' question into (a) a continuous threshold sub-problem (how much / which range) and (b) a discrete multi-criteria choice sub-problem (which method) — solve each with a fitted tool

Use Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation (weight vector A, judgment matrix R, B = A·R) as a fast, defensible multi-criteria decision method when alternatives are scored on soft factors like cost/fitness/time — and stratify the matrices by regime (size class, region) so the recommendation adapts
Define an 'effective' vs 'total' event distinction using a domain threshold (here EMR = 40 J/g) so the model only counts events that actually matter — sharpens both risk and pricing
Validate with a small set of representative, fully-specified numerical scenarios that each carry inputs → intermediate quantities → profit, so judges can audit the pipeline end to end
Mirror the executive-summary + action-recommendation deliverable explicitly for ICM-style problems, addressing a named decision-maker (policymakers) with concrete prioritized actions

Pre-empt criticism with an explicit assumptions list and an honest Strengths/Weaknesses section — naming ignored inclination, estimated costs, and un-modeled tech progress builds credibility rather than hiding gaps
